title: How to Use Global Custom Colors in GoHighLevel — Brand Consistency
description: Learn how to use Global Custom Colors in GoHighLevel to maintain brand consistency. Define colors once, sync everywhere. Perfect for agencies managing multiple clients.
html_content:
Managing brand colors across dozens of client projects is exhausting. Every time a client updates their brand palette, you're manually changing colors in emails, funnels, forms, landing pages, and calendars. One hex code change can take hours to propagate everywhere. GoHighLevel's Global Custom Colors feature solves this problem entirely—define your brand colors once, and they sync everywhere automatically. In this guide, I'll show you exactly how to set this up, why it matters for agency efficiency, and how to leverage it across all your builders. Ready to streamline your workflow? Grab a free 30-day trial of GoHighLevel to test this feature with your own clients.

📋 In This Guide

  • What Are Global Custom Colors in GoHighLevel?
  • How to Set Up Global Custom Colors in Brand Boards
  • Importing and Applying Colors Across All Builders
  • Two-Way Syncing: The Game-Changer for Agencies
  • Real-World Use Cases for Brand Consistency

What Are Global Custom Colors in GoHighLevel?

Global Custom Colors are a centralized color management system within GoHighLevel's Brand Boards feature. Instead of manually entering hex codes every time you design an email, funnel, or form, you define your brand's color palette once at the global level. These colors then become available everywhere—across the email builder, funnel builder, form builder, landing page editor, and calendar designs.

Here's the real power: when a client requests a color change, you update it in one place, and it instantly reflects across every asset. No more hunting through 15 different projects to find where you used that specific shade of blue. No more inconsistent branding across marketing channels.

This feature is built into the Brand Boards area of GoHighLevel, which is already your centralized hub for brand guidelines, logos, fonts, and now—colors. It's designed specifically for agencies managing multiple clients, each with their own brand standards.

💡 Pro Tip

Global Custom Colors work best when you align them to your client's official brand guidelines. Request the hex codes for primary, secondary, accent, and neutral colors upfront—it saves questions later and prevents brand drift.

How to Set Up Global Custom Colors in Brand Boards

Setting up Global Custom Colors is straightforward. Here's the step-by-step process:

Step 1: Navigate to Brand Boards
Inside your GoHighLevel account, go to Settings and look for the Brand Boards option. This is your command center for all brand assets.

Step 2: Access the Colors Section
Within Brand Boards, you'll see a dedicated Colors section. Click into it to start defining your custom colors. You can create as many color variables as your brand needs—typically 5–10 colors cover most use cases (primary, secondary, accent, success, warning, error, etc.).

Step 3: Add Your Brand Colors
For each color, you'll input the hex code and give it a clear name. Use descriptive names like "Primary Brand Blue" or "CTA Button Green" so team members instantly know where and why to use each color. This naming convention prevents confusion and maintains consistency across all projects.

Step 4: Assign Color Variables
Once you've defined your colors, assign them to specific roles across your designs. For example, your Primary Brand Blue might automatically fill all primary buttons, while your accent color fills secondary CTAs. This automation ensures consistency without thinking.

Step 5: Save and Activate
Once you've saved your color palette, GoHighLevel makes these colors available across all builders immediately. No additional steps required—your colors are now live.

Importing and Applying Colors Across All Builders

Once your Global Custom Colors are defined in Brand Boards, using them is effortless across all GoHighLevel builders.

In the Email Builder: When designing a campaign email, click the color picker for any element (text, background, buttons). Instead of entering a hex code manually, you'll see your custom colors appear as quick-select options. Click the color you need, and it applies instantly.

In the Funnel Builder: The same applies to pages, buttons, and section backgrounds. Your brand colors are always one click away, eliminating the need to remember hex codes or hunt through design files.

In the Form Builder: Buttons, field backgrounds, labels—all use the same color palette. This ensures forms match the landing pages they appear on.

In Landing Page Editor: Design hero sections, CTAs, and accent elements using your predefined palette. Consistency is automatic.

In Calendar Designs: Event types, section backgrounds, and highlights all pull from your global colors.

The workflow is identical everywhere: open a color picker, select from your custom palette, and move on. No more copying and pasting hex codes or second-guessing what shade of blue you used last month.

Two-Way Syncing: The Game-Changer for Agencies

Here's where Global Custom Colors become truly powerful for agencies: two-way syncing.

When you update a color in Brand Boards, it instantly syncs across every asset where that color was applied—emails, funnels, forms, landing pages, calendars. But it works the other direction too. If you're building a funnel and realize a color needs adjustment for better contrast or brand alignment, you can update it in the funnel builder, and GoHighLevel automatically syncs that change back to your global color palette.

This bidirectional syncing saves agencies enormous amounts of time. A client requests a brand refresh? Update five hex codes in Brand Boards and watch your entire portfolio of designs update in real-time. No manual revisions. No missed assets. No inconsistencies.

For agencies managing 10, 20, or 50+ active clients, this feature compounds your time savings dramatically. What used to take 2–3 hours per brand refresh now takes 5 minutes.

💡 Pro Tip

Create a separate Brand Board for each client with their own custom color palette. This prevents accidentally applying Client A's colors to Client B's designs and keeps your account organized as you scale.

Real-World Use Cases for Brand Consistency

Multi-Channel Campaign Launches: Your client needs email, SMS, landing page, and form designs for a campaign launch. Instead of manually matching colors across four different builders, create the campaign once with Global Custom Colors. Everything matches automatically.

Seasonal Brand Updates: A client wants holiday-themed colors for December. Update the palette in Brand Boards, and every active design—emails scheduled to send, live landing pages, active funnels—updates instantly. No delays, no manual intervention.

A/B Testing Color Variants: You want to test whether a different accent color drives higher CTR. Instead of creating entirely new designs, update the custom color for your test group, measure results, and roll back or adopt the change. The entire test takes minutes to set up.

White-Label Client Designs: If you're building designs for clients to review or present to their stakeholders, Global Custom Colors ensure everything reflects their brand perfectly—and any feedback about color adjustments is implemented in seconds.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I use Global Custom Colors across multiple client accounts?

Global Custom Colors live within a single GoHighLevel account's Brand Boards. If you manage multiple clients under one account (using Brand Boards for each), yes—each client can have their own color palette. If clients have separate accounts, you'd need to set up colors in each account separately.

What happens if I change a global color after it's been applied to designs?

The change syncs automatically across all assets using that color. This is two-way syncing in action. If you don't want a change to apply everywhere, create a new custom color variable instead of modifying the existing one.

How many custom colors can I create per Brand Board?

GoHighLevel doesn't enforce a strict limit, but best practice is 5–10 colors per brand. This keeps your palette clean, forces discipline around brand guidelines, and makes it easier for team members to select the right color quickly.

Do Global Custom Colors work with templates?

Yes. When you create or apply a template, it uses the global colors assigned to it. If you update a global color later, templates using that color update automatically—another time-saver for scaling your agency.

Can team members see and use Global Custom Colors?

Yes, if they have access to the relevant Brand Board. This ensures all team members—designers, copywriters, marketers—are working from the same color palette, eliminating brand inconsistency from human error.
